one_line_takeaway: G2PIA reframes black-box prompt injection as maximizing KL-divergence (proven equivalent to Mahalanobis distance under a Gaussian assumption) and approximates it with a parameter-free cosine-similarity constraint, yielding a query-free, low-cost, generative injection attack that beats mainstream black-box baselines on SQuAD2.0 and Math.
key_finding: Across 7 victim LLMs and 4 Q&A datasets (Table I), the attack is most effective on reading-comprehension data (SQuAD2.0, e.g. ASR 81.46 on gpt-3.5-turbo-0125 and 79.50 on text-davinci-003) and least effective on mathematical reasoning (the hardest to attack). Older/smaller models are more vulnerable (text-davinci-003 and llama-2-7b), while gpt-4-0125-preview is the most robust (e.g. Math ASR 26.49). Against 6 mainstream black-box baselines attacking ChatGPT-3.5 (Table II), G2PIA achieves the best ASR on both SQuAD2.0 (79.50 vs TextFooler 78.59, BertAttack 65.33) and the Math dataset (44.87 vs BertAttack 33.46, TextFooler 26.02), despite being a general, query-free method not designed for math. Ablations (Table III, gpt-3.5-turbo) show the full method substantially beats random-position injection (GSM8K ASR 47.60 vs 29.18) and random component replacement (47.60 vs 18.33). The authors also report insertion position has minimal effect on attack success (Appendix Table IV).
core_contribution: The paper proposes G2PIA (Goal-guided Generative Prompt Injection Attack), a query-free black-box adversarial attack on LLMs. Its central contribution is to replace the heuristic objectives common in prior black-box attacks with a principled goal: maximizing the KL-divergence between the LLM's output distribution on clean text p(y|x) and on adversarial text p(y|x'). The authors prove (Theorem 1) that, under the assumption that both conditional distributions are Gaussian with a shared covariance, maximizing this KL-divergence is equivalent to maximizing the Mahalanobis distance (x'-x)^T Sigma^-1 (x'-x), which they recast as a constrained minimization of ||x'||^2 with closed-form optimum x'* = (Sigma + lambda*I)^-1 * lambda * x. Since Sigma is unknown in a black-box setting, they approximate the solution with a parameter-free cosine-similarity constraint cos(x',x)=gamma. From this they build a concrete generative injection pipeline that produces an input-dependent, semantics-preserving injected sentence rather than modifying words or inserting fixed/garbled triggers.
threat_model: Black-box adversary with access only to the LLM's input/output interface (API-style), no model weights, architecture, gradients, or training data. Crucially the attack is query-free (query-free / boundary-free): it does not iteratively query the victim model to optimize the adversarial text, keeping computational cost low. The attacker's goal is to cause the victim LLM to produce a wrong answer to a Q&A prompt (M(t') != groundtruth r) while (a) preserving the original semantics of the question (D(t',t) < epsilon) and (b) making the injection imperceptible/hard for active defenses to flag. The injected adversarial sentence is mixed into the clean prompt to form the attack prompt.
limitations: The theoretical backbone rests on strong, admittedly unverifiable assumptions for a black-box setting: that p(y|x) is Gaussian with a shared covariance Sigma across clean and adversarial inputs, and that LLM text generation can be treated as classification over an enumerable output space with a bijective text-to-vector map. The authors themselves state (Appendix A) they cannot confirm these hold. The cosine-similarity relaxation is a heuristic approximation to the unsolvable Mahalanobis problem, so the 'goal-guided' guarantee is only approximate. The core-word heuristic assumes the first sentence summarizes the paragraph and relies on subject/predicate/object plus word2vec/WordNet, which is English- and Q&A-specific and may not transfer to other languages, tasks, or open-ended generation. The attack still requires a capable auxiliary LLM (GPT-4-Turbo) to synthesize the injection, so it is not fully self-contained. Absolute ASR is modest against stronger models on reasoning tasks (~24-33% on GPT-4/Llama-70B for Math), and no defense/mitigation evaluation is provided.

datasets_used: Web-based QA (WebQA, crawled from online QA communities)
quantitative_results: Table I (SQuAD2.0 ASR): text-davinci-003 79.50, gpt-3.5-turbo-0125 81.46, gpt-4-0613 72.58, gpt-4-0125-preview 69.34, llama-2-7b-chat 52.13, llama-2-13b-chat 44.33, llama-2-70b-chat 56.31
Table I (Math dataset ASR): text-davinci-003 44.87, gpt-3.5-turbo-0125 29.72, gpt-4-0613 32.00, gpt-4-0125-preview 26.49, llama-2-7b-chat 33.90, llama-2-13b-chat 36.75, llama-2-70b-chat 24.14
Table I (GSM8K ASR): text-davinci-003 48.47, gpt-3.5-turbo-0125 47.60, gpt-4-0613 45.48, gpt-4-0125-preview 43.81, llama-2-7b-chat 38.69

Table II (attacking ChatGPT-3.5): Ours ASR 79.50 (SQuAD2.0) / 44.87 (Math) vs BertAttack 65.33/33.46, TextFooler 78.59/26.02, TextBugger 16.08/18.31, DeepWordBug 6.72/16.83, Stress Test 1.78/8.15, CheckList 3.64/23.41
Table III ablation (gpt-3.5-turbo, GSM8K / Web-based QA ASR): Our Method 47.60 / 42.42; Random position injection 29.18 / 14.20; Random component replacement 18.33 / 11.49
Default hyperparameters: epsilon=0.2, delta=0.05, gamma=0.5; parameter sweep (Table V) confirms (epsilon,gamma)=(0.2,0.5) region gives peak ASR ~46-47 on GSM8K
baselines_compared: MathAttack [31] (referenced comparison: ASR 41.15 with ChatGPT-3.5 on GSM8K)
